level.name: level
level.definition: Loudness above the run's own speed-conditioned smooth-road floor (delta-dB), per band.
indicators: delta-dB: 10*log10(window band energy / per-speed-conditioned floor energy)

detection.measure: event median level_db minus floorAt(band, speed), normalised by LEVEL_NORM_DB=20 into [0,1]

confidence.basis: delta-dB magnitude relative to the per-run noise floor; SNR of the window

discrimination_test.claim: typical smooth-road delta-dB vs worst rough-chop delta-dB
discrimination_test.result: median 2.2-2.7 dB (flat across 0-20 m/s) vs jc4 worst-chop 7-13 dB (peak 13.1 dB)

level.notes: No idle-vs-driving delta-dB pair is recorded in the research (delta-dB is defined relative to each run's own per-speed floor, so an idle stop is close to 0 by construction - that is what 'stop goes quiet' means for tonality, not for level). The discrimination_test above uses the closest real figures: docs/2026-07-04-cross-pass-rough-spot-aggregation-findings.md's typical-vs-worst-chop delta-dB spread, which is the actual empirical evidence for this tag's loudness dimension.

onset-sharpness.name: onset-sharpness
onset-sharpness.definition: How fast an event's envelope attacks - impulsive/transient (e.g. pothole, manhole strike) versus a gradual swell (e.g. rumble strip, sustained texture).
indicators: envelope attack time over a short window

detection.measure: normalised attack slope of the event's chaos/energy rise
detection.provenance: docs/tags/tag-architecture.md starter set; lib/squelch.js envelope analysis

confidence.basis: attack-slope sharpness; accel_cap=0.6 since an accelerometer disambiguates a true impulsive vertical impact from an audio-only sharp attack (e.g. a slammed door)

discrimination_test.claim: onset-sharpness separates impulsive strikes (manhole/pothole) from gradual texture swells

onset-sharpness.notes: Awaits the 82nd/39th Ave rough-road capture with labeled impulsive events; not yet reproduced on the Johnson Creek passes.

sub-bass-ratio.name: sub-bass-ratio
sub-bass-ratio.definition: Fraction of total spectral energy concentrated in the sub-bass band (20-80 Hz) relative to the full band stack, indicating bass-heavy road/engine content versus broadband spread.

detection.measure: subbass band energy divided by total band-stack energy

confidence.basis: SNR of the sub-bass band relative to the per-run noise floor

discrimination_test.claim: sub-bass ratio separates bass-heavy chop (e.g. washboard/gravel) from broadband road noise

sub-bass-ratio.notes: Awaits the 82nd/39th Ave rough-road capture; not yet reproduced on the Johnson Creek passes.

tonality.name: tonality
tonality.definition: Narrow, stable spectral peaks (a harmonic comb) versus broadband noise; the primary discriminator between idle-tonal engine content and moving-rough broadband road content in the sub-bass band.
indicators: narrow stable spectral peaks vs broadband
engine firing fundamental and low harmonics live below 80 Hz

detection.band: sub-bass 20-80 Hz (first-class; adaptive window per band)
detection.measure: HNR = sum(band-bin power where power > localMedian x PEAK_K) / sum(band-bin power), clamped [0,1]; PEAK_K=3
detection.provenance: sub-bass discovery, 2026-07-04 stop-vs-drive test; lib/squelch.js computeSpectralChaos

confidence.basis: peak margin (spectral peak prominence over local median); reliability capped 0 on clipping, near-floor, or speech contamination

tonality.notes: Adjacent 80-250 Hz 'low' band does NOT discriminate (0.72 vs 0.75, broadband either way) - this is why sub-bass is first-class. Near-silence (within BAND_SNR_MIN=6dB of the per-run noise floor) emits confidence 0 rather than a spurious high reading.
